---
title: "ggseg3d walkthrough"
---

```{r}
#| label: wt-setup
#| include: false
knitr::opts_chunk$set(message = FALSE)
library(ggseg3d)
library(dplyr)
dir.create("img", showWarnings = FALSE)
```

ggseg3d turns brain segmentation data into 3D meshes you can rotate, colour, and export.
Version 2.0 replaced the Plotly backend with Three.js — smaller widgets, faster rendering, and a pipe-friendly API that shares atlas objects with the 2D side (ggseg).

This walkthrough covers the full surface area: cortical atlases, subcortical structures, white matter tracts, camera control, colour palettes, edge rendering, and static exports.

## Basic usage

One call gets you an interactive brain:

```{r}
#| label: wt-basic
ggseg3d(hemisphere = "left") |>
  pan_camera("left lateral")
```

Click and drag to rotate, scroll to zoom, right-click to pan.
Hover over any region to see its name.

## Camera positions

`pan_camera()` snaps the camera to standard anatomical views:

```{r}
#| label: wt-camera-preset
#| eval: false
ggseg3d() |>
  pan_camera("left lateral")
```

```{r}
#| label: wt-snap-camera-preset
#| echo: false
ggseg3d() |>
  pan_camera("left lateral") |>
  snapshot_brain("img/wt-camera-preset.png")
```

![Left hemisphere from the lateral side, showing the full extent of frontal, parietal, temporal, and occipital cortex.](img/wt-camera-preset.png "Left lateral camera preset")

The full set of presets:

- `left lateral`, `right lateral`
- `left medial`, `right medial`
- `left superior`, `right superior`
- `left inferior`, `right inferior`
- `left anterior`, `right anterior`
- `left posterior`, `right posterior`

For anything else, pass explicit coordinates:

```{r}
#| label: wt-camera-custom
#| eval: false
ggseg3d() |>
  pan_camera(list(eye = list(x = -200, y = 100, z = 100)))
```

## Plotting your data

The core workflow: bring a data frame with a `region` (or `label`) column that matches the atlas, then map a variable to `colour_by`.
Add `text_by` to surface values in the hover tooltip:

```{r}
#| label: wt-plot-data
some_data <- tibble(
  region = c("precentral", "postcentral", "insula", "superior parietal"),
  p = c(0.01, 0.04, 0.2, 0.5)
)

ggseg3d(.data = some_data, atlas = dk(), colour_by = "p", text_by = "p") |>
  pan_camera("right lateral")
```

Hover over the coloured regions — the tooltip now shows each region's p-value.

## Custom colour palettes

An unnamed vector defines the gradient endpoints:

```{r}
#| label: wt-palette-vector
#| eval: false
ggseg3d(
  .data = some_data,
  atlas = dk(),
  colour_by = "p",
  palette = c("forestgreen", "white", "firebrick")
)
```

A named vector pins colours to specific breakpoints, which lets the scale extend beyond your data range:

```{r}
#| label: wt-palette-named
#| eval: false
ggseg3d(
  .data = some_data,
  atlas = dk(),
  colour_by = "p",
  text_by = "p",
  palette = c("forestgreen" = 0, "white" = .05, "firebrick" = 1)
)
```

```{r}
#| label: wt-snap-palette-named
#| echo: false
some_data <- tibble(
  region = c("precentral", "postcentral", "insula", "superior parietal"),
  p = c(0.01, 0.04, 0.2, 0.5)
)

ggseg3d(
  .data = some_data,
  atlas = dk(),
  colour_by = "p",
  text_by = "p",
  palette = c("forestgreen" = 0, "white" = .05, "firebrick" = 1)
) |>
  snapshot_brain("img/wt-palette-named.png")
```

![Four cortical regions on a green-white-red gradient anchored at 0, 0.05, and 1; the colour bar extends to cover the full 0-1 range.](img/wt-palette-named.png "Named palette with explicit breakpoints")

## Background colour

White works for most journals.
Dark backgrounds work for slides:

```{r}
#| label: wt-background
#| eval: false
ggseg3d() |>
  set_background("black")
```

```{r}
#| label: wt-snap-background
#| echo: false
ggseg3d() |>
  set_background("black") |>
  snapshot_brain("img/wt-background.png")
```

![Both hemispheres of the DK atlas on a black canvas, region colours standing out against the dark background.](img/wt-background.png "Black background for presentations")

## Region edges

> **Experimental.** Edge rendering works in the htmlwidget viewer.
> rgl support (`ggsegray`) is still unreliable across platforms.

`set_edges()` draws outlines along region boundaries:

```{r}
#| label: wt-edges
#| eval: false
ggseg3d(hemisphere = "left") |>
  set_edges("black", width = 10) |>
  pan_camera("left lateral")
```

```{r}
#| label: wt-snap-edges
#| echo: false
ggseg3d(hemisphere = "left") |>
  set_edges("black", width = 10) |>
  pan_camera("left lateral") |>
  snapshot_brain("img/wt-edges.png")
```

![Left hemisphere with thick black lines tracing every region boundary, making the parcellation structure clearly visible.](img/wt-edges.png "Black region boundary edges")

### Grouping edges with edge_by

By default, edges appear wherever adjacent vertices have different colours.
`edge_by` lets you group edges by a different column — useful when you want boundaries between lobes but not between individual regions within the same lobe:

```{r}
#| label: wt-edge-by
#| eval: false
lobe_data <- tibble(
  region = c("precentral", "postcentral", "insula", "superior parietal"),
  p = c(0.2, 0.4, 0.3, 0.5),
  lobe = c("frontal", "parietal", "insula", "parietal")
)

ggseg3d(.data = lobe_data, atlas = dk(), colour_by = "p", edge_by = "lobe") |>
  set_edges("white", width = 1) |>
  set_background("black")
```

```{r}
#| label: wt-snap-edge-by
#| echo: false
lobe_data <- tibble(
  region = c("precentral", "postcentral", "insula", "superior parietal"),
  p = c(0.2, 0.4, 0.3, 0.5),
  lobe = c("frontal", "parietal", "insula", "parietal")
)

ggseg3d(.data = lobe_data, atlas = dk(), colour_by = "p", edge_by = "lobe") |>
  set_edges("white", width = 1) |>
  set_background("black") |>
  snapshot_brain("img/wt-edge-by.png")
```

![White edge lines on a black background separating frontal, parietal, and insular lobes; regions within the same lobe share a boundary-free surface.](img/wt-edge-by.png "Lobe-level boundaries only")

## Subcortical structures

The `aseg` atlas covers structures beneath the cortex.
Fade unmatched regions with `na_alpha` and wrap everything in a glass brain:

```{r}
#| label: wt-subcortical
#| eval: false
subcort_data <- tibble(
  region = c("Thalamus", "Caudate", "Hippocampus"),
  p = c(0.2, 0.5, 0.8)
)

ggseg3d(
  .data = subcort_data,
  atlas = aseg(),
  colour_by = "p",
  text_by = "p",
  na_alpha = .5
) |>
  add_glassbrain()
```

```{r}
#| label: wt-snap-subcortical
#| echo: false
subcort_data <- tibble(
  region = c("Thalamus", "Caudate", "Hippocampus"),
  p = c(0.2, 0.5, 0.8)
)

ggseg3d(
  .data = subcort_data,
  atlas = aseg(),
  colour_by = "p",
  text_by = "p",
  na_alpha = .5
) |>
  add_glassbrain() |>
  snapshot_brain("img/wt-subcortical.png")
```

![Thalamus, caudate, and hippocampus coloured by p-value, floating inside a translucent cortical shell that provides spatial context.](img/wt-subcortical.png "Subcortical atlas with glass brain overlay")

The glass brain is just a translucent cortical surface — enough anatomy to orient the viewer without hiding the structures underneath.

## White matter tracts

The `tracula` atlas renders white matter pathways as tube meshes.
A near-invisible glass brain keeps things oriented:

```{r}
#| label: wt-tracula-basic
#| eval: false
ggseg3d(atlas = tracula()) |>
  add_glassbrain(opacity = 0.1)
```

```{r}
#| label: wt-snap-tracula-basic
#| echo: false
ggseg3d(atlas = tracula()) |>
  add_glassbrain(opacity = 0.1) |>
  snapshot_brain("img/wt-tracula-basic.png")
```

![Major white matter tracts rendered as coloured tubes, visible through a faint cortical outline.](img/wt-tracula-basic.png "TRACULA atlas with glass brain")

### Mapping data to tracts

Data mapping works the same way as cortical and subcortical atlases — the `region` column matches tract names:

```{r}
#| label: wt-tracula-data
#| eval: false
tract_data <- tibble(
  region = c("arcuate fasciculus", "corticospinal tract",
             "cingulum bundle", "uncinate fasciculus"),
  fa = c(0.45, 0.55, 0.40, 0.35)
)

ggseg3d(
  .data = tract_data,
  atlas = tracula(),
  colour_by = "fa",
  text_by = "fa"
) |>
  add_glassbrain(opacity = 0.1)
```

```{r}
#| label: wt-snap-tracula-data
#| echo: false
tract_data <- tibble(
  region = c("arcuate fasciculus", "corticospinal tract",
             "cingulum bundle", "uncinate fasciculus"),
  fa = c(0.45, 0.55, 0.40, 0.35)
)

ggseg3d(
  .data = tract_data,
  atlas = tracula(),
  colour_by = "fa",
  text_by = "fa"
) |>
  add_glassbrain(opacity = 0.1) |>
  snapshot_brain("img/wt-tracula-data.png")
```

![Four white matter tracts coloured on a continuous scale by fractional anisotropy values, with unmatched tracts in grey.](img/wt-tracula-data.png "Tracts coloured by FA values")

### Orientation colouring

`tract_color = "orientation"` encodes fibre direction as RGB — red for left-right, green for anterior-posterior, blue for superior-inferior.
This is the same convention used in DTI colour maps:

```{r}
#| label: wt-tracula-orientation
#| eval: false
ggseg3d(atlas = tracula(), tract_color = "orientation") |>
  add_glassbrain(opacity = 0.1) |>
  set_background("black")
```

```{r}
#| label: wt-snap-tracula-orientation
#| echo: false
ggseg3d(atlas = tracula(), tract_color = "orientation") |>
  add_glassbrain(opacity = 0.1) |>
  set_background("black") |>
  snapshot_brain("img/wt-tracula-orientation.png")
```

![Tracts coloured by fibre orientation — red for left-right, green for anterior-posterior, blue for superior-inferior — against a black background.](img/wt-tracula-orientation.png "DTI-style orientation colouring")

### Tube appearance

`tube_radius` controls thickness, `tube_segments` controls smoothness.
Higher segments look better but render slower:

```{r}
#| label: wt-tracula-tube
#| eval: false
ggseg3d(atlas = tracula(), tube_radius = 4, tube_segments = 16) |>
  add_glassbrain(opacity = 0.1)
```

## Saving images

`snapshot_brain()` renders the widget to a PNG using a headless browser (Chrome or Chromium required):

```{r}
#| label: wt-snapshot
#| eval: false
ggseg3d() |>
  pan_camera("left lateral") |>
  snapshot_brain("brain_lateral.png")
```

### Publication-quality settings

For print, crank up the resolution:

```{r}
#| label: wt-snapshot-hires
#| eval: false
ggseg3d(.data = some_data, atlas = dk(), colour_by = "p") |>
  pan_camera("left lateral") |>
  set_background("white") |>
  snapshot_brain(
    "figure1_brain.png",
    width = 1200,
    height = 1000,
    zoom = 3,
    delay = 2
  )
```

- **width/height** — output dimensions in pixels
- **zoom** — resolution multiplier (2-3 for print)
- **delay** — seconds to wait for the renderer to finish

### Multi-panel figures

Snapshot each view separately, then stitch them together with magick:

```{r}
#| label: wt-multi-panel
#| eval: false
library(magick)

base_plot <- ggseg3d(.data = some_data, atlas = dk(), colour_by = "p") |>
  set_background("white")

base_plot |>
  pan_camera("left lateral") |>
  snapshot_brain("left_lat.png")
base_plot |>
  pan_camera("left medial") |>
  snapshot_brain("left_med.png")
base_plot |>
  pan_camera("right lateral") |>
  snapshot_brain("right_lat.png")
base_plot |>
  pan_camera("right medial") |>
  snapshot_brain("right_med.png")

panels <- image_read(c(
  "left_lat.png",
  "left_med.png",
  "right_lat.png",
  "right_med.png"
))
combined <- image_montage(panels, geometry = "600x500", tile = "2x2")
image_write(combined, "figure1_all_views.png")
```

```{r}
#| label: wt-snap-multi-panel
#| echo: false
some_data <- tibble(
  region = c("precentral", "postcentral", "insula", "superior parietal"),
  p = c(0.01, 0.04, 0.2, 0.5)
)

base_plot <- ggseg3d(.data = some_data, atlas = dk(), colour_by = "p") |>
  set_background("white")

panel_files <- c(
  "img/wt-panel-left-lat.png",
  "img/wt-panel-left-med.png",
  "img/wt-panel-right-lat.png",
  "img/wt-panel-right-med.png"
)

base_plot |> pan_camera("left lateral") |> snapshot_brain(panel_files[1])
base_plot |> pan_camera("left medial") |> snapshot_brain(panel_files[2])
base_plot |> pan_camera("right lateral") |> snapshot_brain(panel_files[3])
base_plot |> pan_camera("right medial") |> snapshot_brain(panel_files[4])

panels <- lapply(panel_files, png::readPNG)
png("img/wt-multi-panel.png", width = 1200, height = 1000)
par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = c(0, 0, 0, 0), oma = c(0, 0, 0, 0))
for (panel in panels) {
  plot.new()
  rasterImage(panel, 0, 0, 1, 1)
}
dev.off()

file.remove(panel_files)
```

![Four views of the same data — left lateral, left medial, right lateral, right medial — stitched into a 2x2 grid.](img/wt-multi-panel.png "Multi-panel figure combining four anatomical views")

## Legend control

```{r}
#| label: wt-legend
#| eval: false
ggseg3d() |>
  set_legend(FALSE)
```

## Widget dimensions

```{r}
#| label: wt-dimensions
#| eval: false
ggseg3d() |>
  set_dimensions(width = 800, height = 600)
```

## Flat shading

Lighting adds depth but shifts colours.
`set_flat_shading()` turns off all lighting so every vertex renders at its exact assigned colour — useful for atlas illustrations or mask extraction:

```{r}
#| label: wt-flat-shading
#| eval: false
ggseg3d() |>
  set_flat_shading()
```

```{r}
#| label: wt-snap-flat-shading
#| echo: false
ggseg3d(hemisphere = "left") |>
  set_flat_shading() |>
  pan_camera("left lateral") |>
  snapshot_brain("img/wt-flat-shading.png")
```

![Both hemispheres with flat shading — uniform colour per region, no highlights or shadows, colours exactly matching the atlas palette.](img/wt-flat-shading.png "Flat shading for exact colour reproduction")

## Orthographic projection

Perspective projection makes distant regions look smaller.
Orthographic projection removes that depth cue, so every region appears at the same scale regardless of distance from the camera:

```{r}
#| label: wt-orthographic
#| eval: false
ggseg3d() |>
  set_orthographic()
```

```{r}
#| label: wt-snap-orthographic
#| echo: false
ggseg3d(hemisphere = "left") |>
  set_orthographic() |>
  pan_camera("left lateral") |>
  snapshot_brain("img/wt-orthographic.png")
```

![Both hemispheres in orthographic projection — regions appear the same size whether they face the camera or sit behind the brain.](img/wt-orthographic.png "Orthographic projection removes perspective distortion")

## Same atlas for 2D and 3D

`ggseg_atlas` objects work with both ggseg (2D polygons) and ggseg3d (3D meshes).
One atlas, two views:

```{r}
#| label: wt-same-atlas
#| eval: false
library(ggseg)

plot(dk())

ggseg3d(atlas = dk())
```
